explanation: This requirement says that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 should also be true at that point. In LTL, this is typically translated as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1.'
The statement means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 should hold as well. In LTL, this is typically encoded by globally insisting that prop_0 implies prop_1.
Whenever prop_0 holds, it should be followed by prop_1, i.e., prop_1 must also hold in every state where prop_0 holds. Globally, wherever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true (implication).

This requirement states that whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' should also hold at that moment. This indicates an invariant relationship where the truth of 'prop_0' implies the truth of 'prop_1' globally. In LTL, this is represented using the G (globally) operator and logical implication.
This specification demands that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This means, globally, if prop_0 is true at some time, prop_1 must also be true at that time.
The requirement means that whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' should also be true at the same time. This is a safety property, typically translated as globally, 'prop_0 implies prop_1'.

This specification means that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 should also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is expressed by stating that globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true as well.
This requirement states that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 should also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is a safety requirement, typically represented as 'Globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.

This requirement states that if 'prop_0' holds at any point, then 'prop_1' should also hold at that same point. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that globally (always), whenever 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' must be true as well.

This requirement states that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 should also hold. This is a safety property: it forbids any situation where prop_0 is true but prop_1 is not. In LTL, this is expressed globally (G), as it should always be the case.
